children.value: fits the data by integrating over possible parameter values under the prior:

children.html: p(x∣Mi)=∫p(θ∣Mi) p(x∣θ,Mi) dθ=Ep(θ∣Mi) ⁣[p(x∣θ,Mi)].p(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathcal{M}_i) = \int p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathcal{M}_i) \, p(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathcal{M}_i) \, d\boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbb{E}_{p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathcal{M}_i)}\!\left[ p(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathcal{M}_i) \right].p(x∣Mi​)=∫p(θ∣Mi​)p(x∣θ,Mi​)dθ=Ep(θ∣Mi​)​[p(x∣θ,Mi​)].

children.value: If the prior concentrates mass on parameters that assign high likelihood to the data, the marginal likelihood is large. If the prior is diffuse over many parameter values, the marginal likelihood is lower — even if the maximum likelihood is high.
This gives the marginal likelihood a built-in

property: more flexible models can assign probability to many datasets, but since each model’s distribution must normalize, no single dataset receives very high probability. Simpler models that focus probability on fewer datasets can assign higher marginal likelihood to those datasets — provided the data actually came from such a model.

children.value: , a fully Bayesian prediction integrates over all models:

p(x_\text{new} \mid \mathbf{x})
= \sum_{i=1}^M p(\mathcal{M}_i \mid \mathbf{x}) \, p(x_\text{new} \mid \mathcal{M}_i, \mathbf{x})
\propto \sum_{i=1}^M p(\mathcal{M}_i) \, p(\mathbf{x} \mid \mathcal{M}_i) \, p(x_\text{new} \mid \mathcal{M}_i, \mathbf{x}).

reduces variance by using a proposal

children.value: The optimal proposal is the posterior itself (giving zero variance), but that is not available in practice.
constructs a good proposal by defining a sequence of distributions that anneal from the prior to the posterior:

children.html: ft(θ)∝p(θ∣Mi) p(x∣θ,Mi)βt,0=βT<βT−1<⋯<β0=1.f_t(\boldsymbol{\theta}) \propto p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid \mathcal{M}_i) \, p(\mathbf{x} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathcal{M}_i)^{\beta_t}, \qquad 0 = \beta_T < \beta_{T-1} < \cdots < \beta_0 = 1.ft​(θ)∝p(θ∣Mi​)p(x∣θ,Mi​)βt​,0=βT​<βT−1​<⋯<β0​=1.

children.value: Samples are propagated through this sequence via MCMC transition operators, yielding an importance weight that provides an unbiased estimate of the marginal likelihood.

children.html: ϕ∗,ν∗=arg⁡max⁡ p(x∣ϕ,ν)=arg⁡max⁡∫p(x∣θ) p(θ∣ϕ,ν) dθ. oldsymbol{\phi}^*, u^* = \arg\max \, p(\mathbf{x} \mid oldsymbol{\phi}, u) = \arg\max \int p(\mathbf{x} \mid oldsymbol{ heta}) \, p( oldsymbol{ heta} \mid oldsymbol{\phi}, u) \, d oldsymbol{ heta}.ϕ∗,ν∗=argmaxp(x∣ϕ,ν)=argmax∫p(x∣θ)p(θ∣ϕ,ν)dθ.

children.value: For exponential families this objective is available in closed form; for more complex models, the Laplace approximation or other methods can be used. Optimization is typically done via gradient descent.
Bayesian model comparison via the marginal likelihood requires a
. In the improper/uninformative limit, the marginal likelihood is zero.

children.value: If the model is well-specified, the observed data should look like a plausible draw from the posterior predictive distribution.

children.html: y∼N(μ,σ2)y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)y∼N(μ,σ2)

children.value: with a flat prior, we can generate replicated datasets and compare their histograms to the original data. Systematic discrepancies indicate model misspecification.
Rather than comparing full datasets, it is often easier to compare

children.value: for the observed data under each posterior draw.

T(y,θ)=min⁡(y)T(y, \boldsymbol{\theta}) = \min(y)T(y,θ)=min(y)

children.value: for Newcomb’s data reveals that the Gaussian model fails to capture the outliers in the left tail — the minimum of the observed data is far smaller than the minimum of any replicated dataset.

children.html: ppp

children.value:
p = \Pr\!\left(T(y^\text{rep}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \geq T(y, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \mid y\right)
= \int\!\!\int \mathbb{I}\!\left[T(y^\text{rep}, \boldsymbol{\theta}) \geq T(y, \boldsymbol{\theta})\right] p(y^\text{rep} \mid \boldsymbol{\theta}) \, p(\boldsymbol{\theta} \mid y) \, dy^\text{rep} \, d\boldsymbol{\theta}.

children.html: ppp

children.value: Beyond goodness-of-fit, it is important to assess how sensitive conclusions are to modeling choices:

children.html: ttt

children.value: distribution instead of Gaussian for robustness to outliers, hierarchical instead of pooled models).
: vary prior hyperparameters and check that key inferential conclusions are stable.
: extreme quantiles and extrapolations are more sensitive than means and interpolations.
